t: Evidence from history, physical examination, or laboratory results: symptoms are a direct consequence of medication, substance, or medical illness.
Montejo A.L. et al. — SSRI-induced dysfunction.
EMA (2019) — included PSSD in SSRI label.
Medical and endocrine assessment (testosterone, prolactin, glucose, HbA1c, lipids, thyroid).
There is no medical or pharmacological evidence.
Sexual dysfunction persisting after SSRI discontinuation.
FSFI, IIEF, ASEX (Arizona Sexual Experience Scale).
Medication-related — dose reduction, medication switch (bupropion, mirtazapine, vortioxetine, aripiprazole — in antidepressant or antipsychotic strategies);
Adjunct pharmacotherapy — sildenafil (PDE5) in ED and SSRI-induced female dysfunction (Nurnberg JAMA 2008); bupropion adjunct.
In hyperprolactinemia — antipsychotic switch (aripiprazole, quetiapine) or dose modification; dopamine agonist only with endocrine assistance.
Psychotherapy and couple therapy — in psychogenic component.
Patient education and informed consent — prior to initiating antidepressants and antipsychotics, it is important to provide information about sexual side effects.
PSSD — no specific validated treatment exists; clinical support and monitoring; research ongoing.
Montejo A.L. et al. — antidepressant sexual side effect algorithms.
NICE and APA guidelines — management of antidepressant sexual side effects.
SSRI-induced dysfunction (male and female); Nurnberg JAMA RCT.
Most cases improve with etiological management and medication switch; PSSD may rarely persist.
Myth 1: "Antidepressant sexual side effects are 'temporary' and require no intervention"
Montejo studies — side effects are persistent (30–70%); require active management.
Myth 2: "PSSD is not a clinical entity"
EMA 2019 — PSSD risk included in SSRI label; clinically recognized entity.
Myth 3: "Antipsychotic sexual dysfunction is the same for everyone"
risperidone and paliperidone high hyperprolactinemia risk; aripiprazole and quetiapine less; switching is effective.
Myth 4: "Sexual dysfunction due to a medical illness is only a consequence of the illness, no intervention exists"
In diabetic ED, PDE5 inhibitors are effective; sexual function improves with cardiovascular rehabilitation; active intervention is important.
Myth 5: "It is not necessary to discuss sexual side effects before starting an antidepressant"
informed consent is ethical and clinical requirement; later emerging side effects lead to patient distrust and reduced treatment adherence.
WHO. ICD-11. HA05 Sexual dysfunction associated with disorder/disease classified elsewhere. 2024.
Montejo A.L. et al. Frequency of sexual dysfunction and other reproductive side-effects in patients treated with psychotropic drugs. J Sex Marital Ther 2018;44(4):361–371.
EMA. PRAC recommends new product information warnings on sexual dysfunction after SSRI/SNRI treatment. 2019.
Nurnberg H.G. et al. Sildenafil treatment of women with antidepressant-associated sexual dysfunction. JAMA 2008;300(4):395–404.
Bahrick A.S. Persistence of sexual dysfunction side effects after discontinuation of antidepressant medications. Open Psychol J 2008;1:42–50.
